Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
South Asian Traditions

Dignāga

c. 480 – 540 CE

Perception, inference, and apoha become a sharply articulated Buddhist theory of cognition.

1. Historical and textual position

Dignāga belongs to a decisive moment in Indian philosophy. Earlier Buddhist philosophy, especially Abhidharma and Yogācāra, had already analyzed cognition in terms of momentary mental events, mental factors, causal series, and the absence of an enduring self. Dignāga inherits that Buddhist psychological and metaphysical background, but he transforms it into an explicitly argumentative system that can compete with Nyāya, Mīmāṃsā, Vaiśeṣika, Sanskrit grammarians, and other non-Buddhist schools on shared philosophical terrain. The Oxford Research Encyclopedia summarizes the transformation by saying that Dignāga and Dharmakīrti took an earlier, more intramural Buddhist tradition and turned it into a cross-sectarian philosophical enterprise centered on pramāṇa, reliable cognition or epistemic warrant.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Dignāga’s work makes cognition itself the central object of philosophical explanation. Classical Indian debates about perception asked whether perception is purely nonconceptual or whether determinate, concept-involving perception is also genuine perception. Buddhists such as Dignāga defended the former; Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā defended forms of realism according to which perception can disclose objects with their universals, properties, and conceptual determinations. The debate over whether perception is concept-free or concept-involving is one of the closest Indian analogues to modern debates over nonconceptual content.

Dignāga’s principal work is the Pramāṇasamuccaya, the “Compendium of Valid Cognition” or “Compendium of the Means of True Knowledge.” It treats perception, inference for oneself, inference for others, logical reasons and examples, language and meaning, and argumentative errors. The text is not fully preserved in Sanskrit as an independent work; much of it is known through Tibetan translations, fragments, and later commentarial materials, including Jinendrabuddhi. This textual situation matters because “Dignāga” in later philosophical history is often a reconstructed Dignāga: partly the historical author, partly the Dignāga transmitted through Dharmakīrti, and partly the Dignāga preserved by Indian, Tibetan, and Chinese exegetes.

A second crucial text is the Ālambanaparīkṣā, “Investigation of the Percept” or “Investigation of the Object of Cognition.” There Dignāga asks what can count as the object or support of perceptual cognition. The text defines the percept as that which both causes perceptual cognition and appears to perceptual consciousness; it then argues that neither macroscopic external objects nor external atoms can satisfy both conditions. Only an internal cognitive object, or a form internal to cognition, can do so. This work is therefore indispensable for reconstructing Dignāga’s philosophy of mind, because it gives his account of intentional objecthood and pushes his epistemology toward a Yogācāra-style internalism or epistemic idealism.

A historical caution is necessary. Dignāga is often paired with Dharmakīrti, but they are not identical. Dignāga defines perception as free from conceptual construction; Dharmakīrti later adds the explicit qualification “non-erroneous.” Dignāga’s apoha theory is more “top-down,” relying on exclusion as the structure of conceptual meaning; Dharmakīrti increasingly reinterprets the theory through causal and pragmatic relations. Many modern summaries of “Buddhist epistemology” blend the two, so a rigorous reconstruction must separate Dignāga’s own architecture from later Dharmakīrtian refinements.

2. The core architecture: two pramāṇas, two domains of mind

Dignāga’s mature epistemology begins with a stark division: there are only two valid cognitive instruments, perception and inference. Perception has particulars as its object; inference has general features or universals as its object. This is not just an epistemological classification. It is a philosophy of mind because it divides mental life into two fundamentally different modes: direct, nonconceptual presentation and mediated, conceptual construction.

The twofold structure looks like this:

Cognitive mode Sanskrit Object Mental character Philosophical role Perception pratyakṣa Particular, svalakṣaṇa Nonconceptual, immediate, pre-linguistic Gives direct contact with what is ultimately real or directly present Inference anumāna General feature, sāmānyalakṣaṇa Conceptual, linguistic, mediated by signs Produces judgment, classification, reasoning, and communicable knowledge Conceptual construction kalpanā / vikalpa Constructed object, kind, property, name Memory- and language-mediated Stabilizes the world of ordinary objects Exclusion apoha “Not non-X” rather than real X-ness Differential semantic operation Explains meaning without real universals Self-awareness svasaṃvedana / svasaṃvitti The cognition itself Reflexive, first-personal Explains subjective access without a self-substance

This division produces Dignāga’s distinctive view of the mind: cognition is not a single uniform relation between a subject and a world. It is a layered event in which bare appearing, reflexive awareness, conceptual determination, linguistic exclusion, and practical success are different strata. Ordinary experience seems unified, but philosophy reveals that it is a composite.

3. Perception: nonconceptual awareness without “seeing-as”

Dignāga’s definition of perception is famously concise: perception is cognition free from conceptual construction, kalpanāpoḍha. The Stanford Encyclopedia entry on Indian perception explains the implication sharply: for Dignāga, perception is not seeing-as; it is awareness of bare particulars without identification, naming, or classification. The moment one sees “this as a cow,” “this as blue,” “this as mine,” or “this as a person,” one has already passed beyond perception strictly understood into conceptual construction.

This has several consequences. First, perception is not propositional. It does not say “this is blue” or “there is a pot.” Second, perception is not linguistic. Words do not enter into perceptual content. Third, perception is not classificatory. It does not apprehend universals such as “cowness,” “blueness,” or “pothood.” Fourth, perception is not memory-dependent; conceptual recognition requires memory, but bare presentation does not. This is why Dignāga rejects the Nyāya-Mīmāṃsā idea that determinate perception, perception already shaped by class concepts, is a kind of genuine perception.

The philosophical significance is immense. Dignāga is an early and radical nonconceptualist. He holds that there is a form of awareness whose epistemic function is not derivative from concepts. But he is not simply saying that perception is “raw sensation” in the modern empiricist sense. Perception is not a meaningless blur awaiting conceptual interpretation; it is a valid cognition in its own right, but its validity is restricted to particulars. It discloses the particular as present, not as an instance of a type.

In contemporary terms, Dignāga would reject the idea that perceptual experience is already conceptually articulated all the way down. But he would also resist the idea that perception has the kind of representational content that contemporary philosophers often call “nonconceptual content.” His point is more austere: direct perception is a mode of presentation prior to the imposition of content expressible in judgments. Contemporary nonconceptual-content theory says that some mental states can represent the world even when the subject lacks the relevant concepts; Dignāga’s position is stronger and stranger, because he restricts ultimate perceptual disclosure to ineffable particulars.

4. Particulars, universals, and the ontology of cognitive objects

The metaphysics behind Dignāga’s philosophy of mind is Buddhist nominalism. What is ultimately real is not a world of enduring substances bearing repeatable properties. What is ultimately real, for this tradition, is the momentary particular, svalakṣaṇa. A particular is not an instance of a universal; it is unique, causally specific, and inexpressible. Universals are not perceived features of the world. They are conceptual constructions.

This is where Dignāga diverges sharply from Nyāya realism. For the Naiyāyika, perception can disclose real objects as qualified by real universals and properties. When one sees a cow, one can perceptually apprehend not merely a particular sensory event but a real cow possessing cowhood. For Dignāga, this confuses two mental operations. The particular is perceptually presented; “cow” is conceptually imposed. The realist treats conceptual success as evidence that universals exist; Dignāga treats conceptual success as evidence that exclusion and inference work well enough for practical life.

The Buddhist background includes no-self, momentariness, and the denial that conceptual structures carve reality at its ultimate joints. The SEP entry on Indian perception explicitly characterizes Dignāga’s position as idealist-nominalist in spirit and tied to Buddhist doctrines such as no-self and the evanescence of all existents. It also notes that Yogācāra idealism is contested in modern scholarship: some interpret it as metaphysical idealism, others as epistemic, phenomenological, or phenomenalist. This caution is important, because Dignāga need not be read as a crude solipsist.

The mind-theoretical result is that ordinary object perception is a construction. What we call “seeing a pot” involves at least two layers: a nonconceptual presentation of a particular and a conceptual act that classifies, names, and stabilizes it as a pot. The everyday world of persistent objects, persons, kinds, and properties is not simply given; it is built.

5. The object of cognition: Dignāga’s internalism

The Ālambanaparīkṣā deepens the theory. Dignāga asks: what is the object of perception? The answer cannot simply be “external objects,” because an object of perception must both cause the cognition and appear in the cognition. Macroscopic external objects, on the Buddhist analysis, are composites and lack ultimate causal efficacy; external atoms, even if causally effective, do not appear as such in perception. Therefore, Dignāga concludes that only an internal cognitive object can serve both roles.

This is one of the most important points for philosophy of mind. Dignāga’s account is neither simple naïve realism nor simple Cartesian representationalism. It is not naïve realism because the immediately available object is not a mind-independent object just as it is. But it is not Cartesian dualism either, because Dignāga does not posit a thinking substance enclosed behind ideas. The “object” is a form or appearance within a moment of cognition, and that cognition is itself part of a causal stream.

This gives Dignāga an early theory of intentionality. A cognition is “of” an object not because a self-substance looks outward toward a world, but because cognition has an appearing aspect and causal conditions. The perceptual object is what is phenomenally present and causally operative in the cognitive event. Later Buddhist epistemology develops increasingly refined distinctions between the object as directly apprehended and the object as conceptually determined, but the seed is already visible in Dignāga.

The Western comparison is instructive. Descartes’s theory of ideas often appears to place internal ideas between mind and world; some interpreters call this a “veil of perception” problem. Dignāga also internalizes the immediate perceptual object, but without the Cartesian ontology of a thinking substance distinct from extended matter. The resulting problem is similar, though: if the immediately given is internal to cognition, how do we account for the mind’s relation to a world beyond cognition?

6. Self-awareness: subjectivity without a subject-substance

Dignāga’s most important contribution to philosophy of mind may be svasaṃvedana, self-awareness or reflexive awareness. The core idea is that cognition does not need a second cognition to become aware of itself. A cognition is self-presenting. When one sees blue, the cognitive event presents blue and is also aware of itself as a seeing. This self-awareness is not an additional inner observer; it is an intrinsic aspect of the cognitive event. Kellner’s close study of Dignāga notes that the concept of self-awareness enters Buddhist epistemological discourse in the Pramāṇasamuccaya and that it gives subjective access to one’s own mental states and factors.

This doctrine solves several problems at once. It explains first-person access: I do not infer that I am seeing from external evidence. It explains memory: later I can remember not only blue but that I saw blue. It avoids regress: if awareness of a cognition required a second cognition, then awareness of the second would require a third, and so on. It also preserves Buddhist no-self: the subjectivity of experience does not require a permanent subject.

The distinction is subtle but crucial. Dignāga is not saying that every cognition contains an ego. He is saying that every cognition is luminous to itself. Subjectivity is a feature of the event, not the property of an enduring self. A person is a constructed continuity of such events; the first-personal character of experience does not imply an ātman.

This makes Dignāga strikingly close to some phenomenological views of pre-reflective self-consciousness. Phenomenologists often distinguish reflective introspection from the immediate first-personal givenness of experience. The SEP entry on phenomenological self-consciousness says prereflective self-consciousness is implicit, first-order, and present whenever one is undergoing an experience; it is not a second-order act of inspecting oneself. That is very close structurally to Dignāga’s svasaṃvedana, though the metaphysical frameworks differ.

It also contrasts with higher-order theories of consciousness, according to which a mental state becomes conscious by being represented by a higher-order state. Dignāga’s view is closer to same-order or self-representational accounts: consciousness is not conferred from outside by a distinct monitoring state; reflexivity is built into the cognitive episode itself.

7. Conceptual construction: how mind manufactures the ordinary world

If perception is nonconceptual, then most of what we ordinarily call experience is not perception in the strict sense. “This is a table,” “that is a friend,” “this color is blue,” “this pain is mine,” “this object is the same one I saw yesterday”—all of these involve conceptual construction. Dignāga’s term kalpanā covers the mind’s imposition of names, classes, relations, and repeatable identities onto what is, at the ultimate level, a stream of particulars.

This is not a minor add-on. For Dignāga, ordinary cognition is conceptually saturated after the first moment of bare presentation. Later Buddhist discussions emphasize that what we take to be continued perception of the same object is actually a sequence in which subsequent similar cognitions are interpreted as a durable object. Funayama’s discussion of the Buddhist pramāṇa tradition states that direct perception exists only momentarily, while succeeding similar cognitions belong to conceptual construction rather than strict direct perception.

This yields a powerful theory of mental construction. The mind does not merely receive the world; it stabilizes it. It overlays temporal continuity onto momentary events. It groups nonidentical particulars under one concept. It interprets causal series as persisting things. It treats bundles of mental and bodily events as persons. It treats remembered and anticipated patterns as real objects of concern. Much of ordinary reality is therefore cognitively fabricated.

The theory is not skeptical in a simple sense. Dignāga does not say that ordinary objects are useless illusions. He says that conceptual constructions are not ultimately real in the way perception’s particulars are. They can still function conventionally. They can guide action, communication, reasoning, and liberation-oriented analysis. This distinction between ultimate and conventional cognitive function is one of the most important features of Buddhist philosophy of mind.

8. Apoha: meaning as exclusion

Dignāga’s theory of language, apoha, is one of the most sophisticated parts of his system. The basic problem is this: if only particulars are real, and if particulars are ineffable, how can words mean anything? “Cow” cannot refer to a universal cowhood, because no such universal exists. Nor can it refer to all individual cows directly, because each particular is unique and momentary. Dignāga’s answer is that a word functions by exclusion: “cow” means not-non-cow. Meaning is not positive reference to a universal but the exclusion of what is incompatible.

Apoha is therefore a theory of conceptual content. Concepts do not mirror universals; they sort by contrast. A concept is a rule for excluding alternatives. To classify something as blue is not to perceive blueness as a real universal but to exclude it from what counts as non-blue. To classify something as a self is not to find a self-substance but to apply a pragmatic and linguistic construction that excludes what is treated as other.

This has major implications for philosophy of mind. First, mental content is differential: what a concept is depends on what it excludes. Second, concepts are not direct copies of percepts. They are inferential and linguistic operations. Third, the mind’s conceptual map is conventional, interest-relative, and action-guiding. Fourth, because concepts are constructed, they can also mislead. They are indispensable for ordinary life but dangerous when reified.

Dignāga’s apoha theory also anticipates problems that became central in modern philosophy of language and mind: how do words connect to the world? How can general terms apply to particulars? Is meaning referential, inferential, differential, or use-based? Dignāga’s answer is radically nominalist: linguistic meaning is not grounded in shared universals but in exclusion patterns that allow practical coordination.

The major problem is the “scheme-content gap.” If perception gives only ineffable particulars and concepts operate through exclusions, how do conceptual schemes remain answerable to reality? The SEP entry on Dharmakīrti describes this as a problem for Buddhist epistemologists: they separate conceptual scheme from perceptual content and must then explain how thought and language are still about reality. Dignāga’s top-down apoha answer was criticized by non-Buddhist realists; Dharmakīrti later tried to solve the problem by giving a more causal and pragmatic account.

9. Inference and the public mind

Dignāga’s second pramāṇa is inference, anumāṇa. Inference is conceptual and mediated by signs. It is not direct presentation of a particular but cognition of a general relation. Dignāga distinguishes inference for oneself from inference for others, thereby distinguishing private reasoning from public argument. In the Hetucakra and related discussions, he analyzes the validity of reasons by examining whether a middle term is present in the subject, present in similar cases, and absent in dissimilar cases.

This gives Dignāga’s philosophy of mind a social dimension. Perception is private in the sense that it is directly present only to the experiencing stream. Inference, by contrast, can be made public. It can be formulated as an argument, tested through examples, and challenged by opponents. The mind is therefore not only a site of private appearances but also a participant in a norm-governed practice of reasons.

The result is a layered epistemic psychology. At the base are nonconceptual perceptual events. Above them are conceptual recognitions and classifications. Above those are inferential structures that can be articulated for oneself or others. Above those are debates, proofs, refutations, and communal norms of rational accountability. Dignāga thus connects philosophy of mind with logic: the mind is not only conscious but answerable to standards of valid cognition.

This is one of the great differences between Dignāga and a purely introspective theory of consciousness. He is not simply describing what experience feels like from within. He is building an account of how cognition becomes warranted, communicable, and corrigible. That is why his philosophy of mind cannot be separated from his logic.

10. Error, illusion, and misclassification

Dignāga’s framework explains error primarily through conceptual construction. Bare perception, strictly construed, does not judge that something is a pot, a cow, water, or a person. Error enters when the mind imposes a conceptual determination that fails. A mirage may present a visual appearance; the false cognition is “there is water.” A rope may present a visual particular; the false cognition is “this is a snake.”

This does not mean Dignāga has an easy theory of illusion. The double-moon case, sensory distortion, and hallucination generate real pressure. If perception is simply nonconceptual presentation, are distorted appearances also perceptions? Later Dharmakīrti adds “non-erroneous” to the definition of perception to exclude such cases, and later commentators interpret non-erroneousness pragmatically as non-deceptiveness in relation to successful action.

The key distinction is between appearing and taking-as. Dignāga wants to preserve the idea that direct presentation is epistemically basic while explaining why ordinary cognition is often false. His answer is that falsity belongs to the conceptual superimposition, not to the bare particular as presented. But this leaves a difficult question: can the perceptual appearance itself be misleading before conceptualization? Dharmakīrti’s addition suggests that Dignāga’s own formulation needed repair.

In philosophy of mind, this is an early version of the problem of perceptual content and illusion. If perception has no conceptual content, how can it be accurate or inaccurate? If it has content, is that content conceptual or nonconceptual? If perceptual experience is internal to cognition, how do hallucination and veridical perception differ? Dignāga’s system anticipates these questions but does not settle all of them.

11. Memory, affect, and inner mental life

Dignāga’s self-awareness doctrine is especially important for memory. When one later remembers seeing blue, one remembers not only the object but the earlier cognition. This would be impossible if the earlier cognition had not been self-aware. Self-awareness therefore plays a structural role in diachronic mental life: it lets the stream of cognition retain access to its own past episodes without positing an enduring self.

The doctrine also extends beyond sensory cognition. Dignāga’s self-awareness concerns mental states and mental factors: desire, pleasure, pain, passion, and conceptual states. The mind is not merely a representational apparatus; it includes affective and conative factors. Self-awareness is the way these mental factors are first-personally present without requiring an inner spectator.

This is where Dignāga’s Buddhist context matters. The point of analyzing cognition is not merely theoretical. Conceptual construction is bound up with attachment, aversion, and ignorance. The self is not simply a metaphysical mistake; it is an affectively charged construction. “I,” “mine,” “enemy,” “desirable,” and “permanent” are conceptual formations with emotional consequences. A Dignāgan philosophy of mind therefore links semantics, affect, and suffering.

12. No-self and the construction of personhood

Dignāga’s philosophy of mind must be read against the Buddhist denial of a permanent self, anātman. Self-awareness does not restore the self under another name. It explains how there can be subjectivity without a subject-substance. Each cognition is self-presenting, but no cognition reveals an enduring owner behind the stream.

This is a distinctive alternative to both substance dualism and eliminative accounts of subjectivity. Dignāga does not say, “There is a self, and it knows its mental states.” Nor does he say, “There is no first-personal awareness.” He says, in effect: there is first-personal awareness, but it belongs to momentary cognitions, not to a metaphysical ego.

The self, on this reconstruction, is an apoha-like conceptual construction over a causal stream. It is useful for ordinary communication and moral-practical life, but it is not ultimately disclosed in perception. “I” is a conventionally functioning designation, not an ultimately real referent. This is one of the most powerful components of the Dignāgan philosophy of mind: it separates subjectivity from selfhood.

The contrast with Descartes is sharp. In the Cartesian tradition, conscious thought becomes evidence for the existence of a thinking subject, and the mind-body problem is framed around the relation between mental and physical substances or properties. The SEP entry on seventeenth-century theories of consciousness notes that for Descartes consciousness becomes a mark of the mental, tied to transparency, reflection, and intentionality; the SEP entry on dualism frames the modern mind-body problem around the relation between mental and physical characteristics. Dignāga shares the concern with first-person access but rejects the move from conscious cognition to enduring subject-substance.

13. Yogic perception and transformed consciousness

Dignāga and the later Buddhist epistemological tradition also recognize the idea of yogic perception, yogipratyakṣa. This is not ordinary sense perception; it is a cultivated nonconceptual cognition arising from meditative practice. The problem is philosophically fascinating: Buddhist truths such as impermanence and suffering are normally expressed as universal judgments, but perception is supposed to apprehend particulars, not universals. How, then, can a yogin nonconceptually perceive truths that seem conceptually structured?

Later Buddhist philosophers wrestle with this issue in detail. One answer is that meditation begins conceptually: one reflects on impermanence, suffering, no-self, or emptiness. Through repeated cultivation, that conceptual understanding is transformed into a vivid, nonconceptual mode of realization. This gives Dignāga’s philosophy of mind a developmental dimension: the mind can move from conceptual belief to nonconceptual insight.

This also shows that Dignāga’s nonconceptualism is not anti-intellectual. Concepts are necessary for ordinary reasoning and even for initiating contemplative practice. But final insight is not merely the possession of correct propositions. It is a transformation in the mode of cognition. Philosophy of mind, epistemology, and soteriology converge: liberation requires changing not only what one believes but how things appear.

14. The reconstructed Dignāgan philosophy of mind

The following theses summarize the philosophy of mind that can be extracted from Dignāga.

First, mind is evental, not substantial. Mental life consists of momentary cognitions and mental factors, not the acts of a permanent self.

Second, consciousness is reflexive. A cognition is not hidden from itself; it is self-aware without needing a second act of introspection.

Third, perception is nonconceptual. Direct perception does not involve naming, classification, predication, or recognition-as.

Fourth, perception is particular-directed. What is directly presented is the particular, not the universal, kind, property, or enduring object.

Fifth, ordinary objects are constructed. The world of pots, cows, persons, selves, and durable things is produced by conceptual synthesis over momentary presentations.

Sixth, language is exclusionary. Words do not refer to real universals; they function by excluding what is incompatible.

Seventh, inference is conceptual and public. Reasoning depends on signs, general relations, and communicable standards of validity.

Eighth, error arises through misconstructed taking-as. The mind overlays a concept, name, or identity onto what is presented; false cognition occurs when that overlay fails.

Ninth, subjectivity does not entail selfhood. There is first-personal givenness, but no enduring metaphysical subject.

Tenth, cognition is pragmatically constrained. Conceptual cognition is not ultimately real, but it can be conventionally correct when it supports successful action and does not mislead. Later Buddhist epistemologists make this pragmatic dimension especially explicit.

15. Dignāga in the larger Indian development

Dignāga’s immediate significance is that he gives Buddhist philosophy a new argumentative form. Earlier Buddhist thought had analyzed experience in great detail, but Dignāga recasts that analysis as a theory of valid cognition and debate. He narrows the accepted sources of knowledge to perception and inference, rejects independent authority for testimony as a separate pramāṇa, and makes logic central to Buddhist intellectual life.

His influence is profound but mediated. Dharmakīrti, c. seventh century, becomes the dominant later figure. In Tibet, Dignāga is revered as a patriarch of Buddhist epistemology, but his thought is often understood through Dharmakīrti’s Pramāṇavārttika. The introduction to Dignāga’s Investigation of the Percept notes that Tibetan scholars generally discussed Dignāga in the context of Dharmakīrti studies and that Dharmakīrti’s work became the standard lens in the Gelukpa tradition.

In East Asia, the transmission differs. Chinese Buddhist traditions received Dignāga’s works without the same Dharmakīrtian overlay, especially through translations and commentaries connected to Dharmapāla, Paramārtha, Xuanzang, and Yijing. This means that there is not one single historical Dignāga-reception but multiple Dignāgas: the Indian Dignāga, the Dharmakīrtian Dignāga, the Tibetan scholastic Dignāga, and the East Asian Yogācāra Dignāga.

Within Indian philosophy, Dignāga’s deepest opponents are realists about universals and determinate perception. Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā philosophers object that if perception gives only bare particulars, it becomes difficult to explain how we ever know ordinary objects, properties, and kinds. They also attack apoha as circular: to understand “non-cow,” must one not already understand “cow”? These objections expose the central pressure point in Dignāga’s system: the transition from nonconceptual presentation to conceptual order.

16. Comparison with parallel Western developments

The earliest Western analogue is not Descartes but ancient Greek psychology. Plato and Aristotle analyze soul, perception, cognition, desire, and the relation of soul to body. Aristotle’s psychology, especially in De Anima, treats the soul through faculties such as nutrition, perception, desire, and mind; his hylomorphism avoids both reductive materialism and Platonic separability. Dignāga is closer to neither Plato nor Aristotle straightforwardly. He is less substance-oriented than Plato and less biological-hylomorphic than Aristotle. His unit of analysis is not the ensouled organism but the momentary cognition.

With Descartes, the convergence is first-person access; the divergence is metaphysical. Descartes treats consciousness as central to the mental and uses conscious thought to secure the thinking subject. Dignāga also treats cognition as self-aware, but he does not infer a thinker behind the thought. Cartesian philosophy intensifies the mind-body problem by distinguishing thinking substance from extended substance; Dignāga dissolves that framing by denying a permanent mental substance in the first place.

With Hume, the convergence is stronger. Hume distinguishes impressions from ideas by force and vivacity and argues that ideas derive from impressions. Dignāga also gives priority to immediate presentation over conceptual thought. Both are suspicious of a substantial self. But Dignāga is not simply a Humean empiricist avant la lettre. Hume’s ideas are copies or recombinations of impressions; Dignāga’s concepts are exclusionary constructions that do not copy universals because universals are unreal.

With Kant, the comparison turns on intuition and concept. Kant’s philosophy famously raises the question of how sensory intuition and conceptual understanding cooperate in experience; contemporary Kant scholarship continues to debate conceptualist and nonconceptualist readings. Dignāga’s answer is more dualistic at the epistemic level: perception and inference have different objects and different mental structures. Kant tries to explain objective experience through the cooperation of sensibility and understanding; Dignāga explains ordinary object-experience as a conventional construction layered onto nonconceptual presentation.

With Brentano and phenomenology, the convergence lies in intentionality and reflexivity. Brentano defines mental phenomena by their directedness toward an object, while phenomenologists later emphasize prereflective self-consciousness. Dignāga’s cognition has both object-presentation and self-presentation, but he does not define mentality solely by object-directedness. His self-awareness doctrine is closer to the phenomenological claim that experience is immediately given to itself than to a model of introspective observation.

With contemporary analytic philosophy, Dignāga is most relevant to debates on nonconceptual content, self-representational consciousness, and the relation between perception and judgment. Modern nonconceptualists ask whether mental states can represent the world without the subject possessing the relevant concepts. Dignāga’s answer is radical: perception is precisely nonconceptual, but what it discloses is not a proposition or a conceptual content; it is the particular prior to classification.

With Sellars and McDowell, Dignāga faces the “myth of the given” problem. If perception is nonconceptual, how can it justify conceptual beliefs? If the given is ineffable, how can it stand in rational relations? Dignāga’s system says that perception constrains thought and that inference structures thought, but the bridge is difficult. Dharmakīrti’s later causal-pragmatic development of apoha can be seen as an attempt to strengthen that bridge.

With cognitive science, Dignāga is suggestive but not directly assimilable. His system resembles a two-stage model: nonconceptual sensory presentation followed by categorization, memory, linguistic labeling, and action-guiding inference. His apoha theory resembles contrastive classification. His self-awareness theory resembles same-order consciousness. But he is not offering a neural or computational theory. His aims are epistemic and soteriological: to explain valid cognition and to undermine reification.

17. Critical assessment

Dignāga’s greatest strength is his integration. He does not treat perception, language, logic, self-awareness, and metaphysics as separate topics. The same basic commitments run through all of them: particulars are directly presented; universals are constructed; cognition is self-aware; concepts are useful but distorting; reason is valid only under disciplined conditions.

His second strength is the separation of subjectivity from ego. Many philosophies assume that first-person awareness requires a self. Dignāga denies this. He gives an account of what it is like for cognition to occur without positing an enduring subject. This is of major contemporary interest because it offers a path between Cartesian ego theories and reductive accounts that struggle to explain first-personal givenness.

His third strength is the diagnosis of conceptual reification. Human beings do not merely perceive; they name, classify, project, stabilize, and cling. Dignāga gives a technical account of how this happens. Apoha is not just a semantic theory; it is also a theory of how the mind builds a world of kinds and then mistakes those kinds for reality.

The main weakness is the bridge problem. If perception is wholly nonconceptual and particulars are ineffable, then the connection between perception and conceptual knowledge becomes fragile. How exactly does the mind move from bare particular to correct classification? How can “not-non-cow” be learned without already possessing some practical grip on cows? How can perception justify inference if perception and inference have different objects? Dignāga’s system identifies the problem with extraordinary clarity, but Dharmakīrti and later commentators do much of the work of answering it.

A second weakness concerns illusion. If perception is defined as free from conceptual construction, then distorted perceptual appearances are hard to classify. Are they perceptions because they are nonconceptual? Or are they excluded because they mislead? Dharmakīrti’s addition of “non-erroneous” indicates that Dignāga’s original definition required further refinement.

A third tension concerns idealism. The Ālambanaparīkṣā pushes toward internal cognitive objects as the immediate objects of awareness. That gives Dignāga a powerful account of appearances, but it raises the external-world problem: how should we understand the status of mind-independent objects? Modern scholars disagree over whether Yogācāra is best read as metaphysical idealism, epistemic idealism, phenomenology, or another form of anti-realism.

18. Final synthesis: Dignāga’s philosophy of mind in one model

A comprehensive Dignāgan model of mind can be stated as follows.

Cognition arises moment by moment. Each cognition is self-aware. Some cognitions present particulars directly; this is perception. These particulars are not yet objects under descriptions. The mind then constructs stable objects through memory, similarity, exclusion, and linguistic classification. Inference organizes these constructions into reasons, judgments, and public arguments. Language functions not by attaching words to real universals but by excluding what falls outside a conventionally useful class. Error occurs when conceptual construction misleads action or reifies what is merely constructed. The self is one such construction: conventionally useful, affectively powerful, but ultimately not found. Liberation requires seeing through the reified products of conceptuality and transforming cognition so that reality is no longer habitually grasped through false universals, permanence, ownership, and ego.

Historically, this makes Dignāga one of the most important premodern philosophers of mind, even though he did not write under that disciplinary label. He anticipates debates about nonconceptual content, self-awareness, intentionality, nominalism, semantic externalism and internalism, perceptual error, the constructed self, and the relation between private awareness and public reasons. His differences from Western philosophy are as important as his similarities: he does not begin from substance, soul, or mind-body dualism, but from valid cognition, momentariness, no-self, and the soteriological danger of conceptual reification.

The central Dignāgan insight is that the mind’s most basic error is not that it fails to think. It is that it thinks too successfully: it builds a coherent world of objects, kinds, selves, and meanings, and then forgets that it built them.

PreviousVasubandhu NextDharmakīrti

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
